questions.question: What does the finally block in a try/catch statement guarantee?
options: It only runs when an error is caught
It always runs, regardless of whether an error was thrown or caught
It runs only when no error occurs
It prevents errors from propagating
questions.answer: It always runs, regardless of whether an error was thrown or caught
questions.explanation: The finally block executes after try (and catch if there was an error), making it ideal for cleanup code like hiding spinners or closing connections.

questions.question: How do you create a custom error class in JavaScript?
options: function CustomError() { this.message = msg; }
class CustomError extends Error { constructor(msg) { super(msg); } }
class CustomError { throw() { throw new Error(); } }
const CustomError = new Error.extend();
questions.answer: class CustomError extends Error { constructor(msg) { super(msg); } }
questions.explanation: Custom errors extend the built-in Error class. You must call super(message) to properly initialize the error, and should set this.name for easy identification.

questions.question: What does the optional chaining operator (?.) do?
options: It makes a property optional in TypeScript
It short-circuits and returns undefined if the left-hand value is null or undefined
It checks if a method exists before calling it
It creates an optional function parameter
questions.answer: It short-circuits and returns undefined if the left-hand value is null or undefined
questions.explanation: user?.address?.city returns undefined instead of throwing a TypeError if user or address is null/undefined, enabling safe property access in chains.

questions.question: How should you handle unhandled Promise rejections globally?
options: Wrap all code in try/catch
Listen for the 'unhandledrejection' event on the window object
Use Promise.allSettled()
Set a global error handler with Promise.onRejection()
questions.answer: Listen for the 'unhandledrejection' event on the window object
questions.explanation: window.addEventListener('unhandledrejection', handler) catches Promise rejections that were not handled with .catch() anywhere in the code.

questions.question: What is the purpose of re-throwing an error in a catch block?
options: To create a new error with a better message
To handle the error in the catch block AND propagate it further
To pass errors that you can't handle locally up to a caller that can handle them
To log the error to the console
questions.answer: To pass errors that you can't handle locally up to a caller that can handle them
questions.explanation: If a catch block handles only specific errors (e.g., ValidationError), it should re-throw unexpected errors so they propagate to a higher-level handler instead of being silently swallowed.
